title: Marbella Restaurants You Can Arrive at by Boat
subtitle: Nine beach clubs and chiringuitos with mooring or tender drop between Puerto Banús and Cabopino, and how to pair lunch with a half-day charter.
author: BoatHire24 Guides

sections.heading: The Fastest Table in Marbella
sections.html:
The fastest table in Marbella is the one you reach by sea. Skip the N-340 traffic, the €25 valet and the 20-minute walk from the car park: arrive directly at the chiringuito by tender, or moor stern-to at a Puerto Banús quayside terrace. This guide covers nine restaurants between Estepona and Cabopino that work for a lunch stop on a half-day charter, the practicalities of getting ashore, and how to pair the meal with a swim at Cala del Faro or Río Verde.

  • 9 lunch-worthy stops between Puerto Banús and Cabopino, all reachable in under 25 minutes by motor yacht.
  • Most chiringuitos sit 50-150m off the beach; a tender shuttle from the anchored yacht takes 3-5 minutes.
  • Spanish lunch service runs 13:30-16:00; reserve a table the morning of your charter.
  • Puerto Banús quay berths are bookable same-day for boats up to 30m via the marina office on VHF 09.
  • Beach clubs charge €30-€60 per sunbed, and arriving by sea skips the road-side parking queue entirely.
  • Average August sea temperature is 22°C, ideal for a swim stop between cocktails and the main course.

sections.heading: Puerto Banús Quayside: Mooring Stern-to for Lunch
sections.html:
Five venues inside the marina take walk-up boat traffic from 12:00 to 16:00. Cipriani (Italian, terrace right on the Levante quay), Astral (Mediterranean fish, opposite the superyacht line), La Sala by the Sea (British-leaning brasserie, easy with kids), Pangea (steaks, lounge atmosphere) and Sumosan (Japanese, end of the harbour). All five sit within a 50m walk of the visitor berths.

Your skipper calls Puerto Banús harbour control on VHF 09 about 30 minutes before arrival and you usually have a berth allocated by the time you tie up. Quayside rates for a 2-3 hour lunch run €40-€90 depending on overall length; for boats over 20m the berth is pre-booked the day before. None of these venues charge a corkage if you arrive by water, so you simply order from their drinks list as usual.

sections.heading: Trocadero Arena and the Río Verde Anchorage
sections.html:
Trocadero Arena sits 1.5NM east of Puerto Banús on the sand at Río Verde. It is the closest serious chiringuito to the marina and the easiest first stop on a 4h Tier A charter at €749. The skipper anchors in 4-5m of water about 100m off the beach, drops the tender, and you step ashore at the lifeguard tower.

The kitchen runs a Mediterranean menu: grilled sea bass €32, ibérico croquettes €18, rice dishes from €28 for two. Sunbeds on their stretch of sand cost €40 a pair including a parasol. Service from 13:30; reserve the morning of your charter on +34 952 779 365. From the anchorage you see La Concha and the Golden Mile framing the coast, one of the best lunch views in Marbella.
sections.image_caption: The Azimut 39 at anchor off the Marbella coast, ready for a tender run ashore

sections.heading: Nikki Beach and the Cabopino Chiringuitos
sections.html:
Nikki Beach Marbella sits 3NM east of Puerto Banús, in front of the Don Carlos hotel. The anchorage is exposed to the east wind, so the skipper checks the forecast and only commits if Levante stays under 12 knots. Tender ashore is a 4-minute run. The dress code matters here: no swimwear at the table after 14:00, so guests change on the boat into linen or a dress before the tender shuttle. A two-course lunch with a bottle of rosé runs €70-€110 a head; sunbeds €60 each. Sunday brunches are theatrical and pricey but a good reason to take the 8h charter at €2,299 and stay for the dancing-on-tables 16:00 set.

Push another 5NM east of Nikki Beach and you reach the dune system at Cabopino. Three chiringuitos work here for a boat lunch: Bono Beach, Andy's Beach and El Oasis. All three are sand-floored, swimwear-OK, with main courses €18-€28 and a kids' menu around €14. Anchorage is sheltered from westerlies and the holding is good in 4m of sand. The skipper drops the hook 120m off Bono Beach's lifeguard post and shuttles you in by tender. This is the best stop if you want a quiet lunch without the Puerto Banús showboat energy, and it pairs nicely with a return swim at Cala Cortés on the way back.

sections.heading: Eating Onboard Instead
sections.html:
If you would rather not go ashore, every charter already includes water, soft drinks, beer, white wine, cava and light snacks, which is enough for a 2-3 hour trip. For a proper meal onboard the crew coordinates with you the day before: tapas platters from €18 per person, sushi boxes around €35, paella for the table at €45 per head.

The order is collected from Puerto Banús before you board and served at anchor in your chosen cove. Drinks remain included on top. This is a good move on August weekends when the popular chiringuitos run two-hour waits for walk-ins.

sections.heading: Recommended Routes by Charter Length
sections.html:
Charter lengthRouteLunch stopPrice (Tier A)
2hPuerto Banús quay loopCipriani or Astral, stern-to€749
4hPuerto Banús to Río Verde and returnTrocadero Arena, tender ashore€1,499
6hPuerto Banús to Nikki Beach, then Cala del Faro swimNikki Beach (smart-casual)€1,899
8hPuerto Banús to Cabopino, sunset returnBono Beach, swimwear€2,299

All four routes run on Tier A boats (Astondoa 40 or Azimut 39, both 12.5m, up to 9-11 guests). Upgrading to a Mangusta 80 for the day starts at €4,719 for 4h and adds a free jet ski, which makes the swim stop between courses considerably more entertaining.

sections.heading: Booking Sequence and Practical Notes
sections.html:
1. Pick a route from the table above and message the date on WhatsApp via boatrentalinmarbella.com.
  2. The boat is held for 24 hours while you reserve the restaurant directly.
  3. Send the reservation confirmation; timings are planned around the table slot.
  4. Pay a 30% deposit to confirm; the balance settles on the day in Puerto Banús.
  5. Arrive 15 minutes before departure at the Levante quay; the skipper greets you with the drinks already on ice.

How the tender drop-off works: the skipper anchors 80-150m off the beach in 4-6m of water, then ferries guests ashore in the tender, two or three at a time. You step off in knee-deep water onto the sand and walk 30 seconds to the chiringuito. Bags, phones and shoes ride in a dry bag, and the skipper collects you when you signal by phone or VHF.

Travelling with children: most chiringuitos are family-friendly. Bono Beach and the Cabopino huts have shaded play areas and kids' menus around €12-€16, while Trocadero Arena and Nikki Beach accept children at lunchtime but lean adult. The boats carry life jackets in every child size from 10kg upwards.
